strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles have evolved across 15 cycles: destination architecture is now open (microservices vs modular monolith vs hybrid), not settled. Strangler Fig with API gateway was the general pattern but is now partially superseded by hybrid destination consideration. No shared databases between services/modules regardless of destination. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker — except that saga/outbox pattern for payments extraction now requires a message broker decision. One team owns each service/module end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning. Test coverage of the 500K LOC monolith is an unassessed risk — newly identified as the most important unknown unknown in cycle 15.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Test coverage of the monolith is unassessed and is the most important unknown unknown entering execution

strands.title: Unknown Unknowns and Residual Risk
strands.content: Cycle 15 strand. Eight risk categories not addressed across 14 prior cycles: (1) Live data migration mechanics — dual-write periods, backfill jobs, cutover validation, data loss/duplication risk at cutover boundary. Every extraction has this problem. Nobody on the team has been confirmed to have zero-downtime live migration experience. (2) Test coverage — the most important unknown unknown. 500K LOC Java apps of sufficient age commonly have poor behavioral coverage. Extraction bugs are invisible without tests; they surface in production. Must be assessed before any extraction begins. Line coverage metrics mislead — behavioral coverage of edge cases, failure paths, and transaction boundary behaviors is what matters. (3) Knowledge concentration — 1-2 people likely hold critical implicit system knowledge. Their absence during migration creates dangerous decision-making from incomplete knowledge. Must be made explicit before execution. (4) Operational gap — team knows monolith operations, not distributed service operations. Runbooks, on-call procedures, incident response playbooks for new services do not exist. This gap surfaces as 2am production incidents, not planning failures. (5) Implicit coupling — static variables, ThreadLocal state, classloader assumptions, Tomcat-specific behaviors, singleton state that accumulates across requests. Invisible to seam analysis. Surfaces as bizarre behavior when code runs in a new JVM context. Shadow mode deployment (duplicate traffic, response comparison) is the best mitigation. (6) Dead code activation — code unreachable in monolith execution context may become reachable in new service context due to different initialization order, class loading, or transaction boundaries. (7) Customer impact of cutover events — forced session invalidation affects end users visibly. Support volume impact and potential churn not planned for. (8) Regulatory change risk — PCI-DSS version changes mid-migration can invalidate architecture decisions. Low probability, high impact. Most actionable immediate items: test coverage assessment (add to archaeology task list), knowledge concentration audit (identify key holders, make knowledge explicit), operational runbook writing as part of definition-of-done for each extraction.

key_claims.text: Test coverage state is the most important unknown unknown — poor coverage makes extraction bugs invisible until production
Live data migration mechanics have not been discussed across 14 cycles despite being a concrete execution risk for every extraction
Implicit coupling (ThreadLocal, static state, classloader assumptions) is invisible to seam analysis and surfaces in production
Knowledge concentration risk — 1-2 people likely hold critical implicit system knowledge whose absence stalls the migration
Operational gap surfaces as 2am incidents, not planning failures — runbooks must be written before go-live, not after
Shadow mode deployment (duplicate traffic, response comparison) is the best available mitigation for implicit coupling

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase and actual requirements before committing to plans that assume something about them. Empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself and reinforced by cycle 13 retrospective: seam analysis recommended cycle 2, unexecuted through cycle 14. Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted. Compliance escalation identified as urgent cycle 4, unresolved. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The architecture pattern question at cycle 11 is a further instance. The cycle 13 retrospective confirmed: initial recommendations were based on category patterns wrong for this specific system. The cycle 14 executive summary operationalized this insight. Cycle 15 adds a new dimension: unknown unknowns expand the verification discipline requirement beyond coupling topology to include test coverage assessment and knowledge concentration audit — both are ground truth questions resolvable by inspection, both have been absent from planning. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
Test coverage assessment and knowledge concentration audit are verification discipline tasks newly identified in cycle 15
Unverified assumptions discovered mid-execution are unrecoverable in a fixed-window migration; discovered in week one they cost a day

strands.title: Epistemic Revision Log
strands.content: Retrospective strand from cycle 13, updated cycle 15. Four confirmed positions from cycle 1 that were materially wrong: (1) Auth-first sequencing — category reasoning ('most Java monoliths'), invalidated cycle 3 by session tokens in payments DB. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — accepted framing without interrogating whether it satisfied actual stated goals. Not challenged until cycle 11. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling not process coupling. (3) Month-by-month plan in cycle 1 was overconfident — no coupling topology, no compliance constraints, no session store location known. Presented with confidence appropriate to verified plan. (4) Team anxiety misread as management problem — the team's nervousness was empirically grounded signal about a system that had already failed. Underlying pattern: confident claims based on category patterns wrong for this specific system. Cycle 14 addition: executive summary applies lessons of this log. Cycle 15 addition: the unknown unknowns strand reveals a fifth category of initial failure — the entire planning conversation never assessed test coverage or knowledge concentration, both of which are as fundamental as coupling topology. The failure mode of planning around unknowns extended to risks the conversation never opened as topics, not just questions it opened but didn't resolve.

key_claims.text: Auth-first sequencing recommendation was based on category reasoning, invalidated by specific fact in cycle 3
Microservices destination should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — a planning error retrospectively confirmed
Test coverage and knowledge concentration were never raised as planning topics — a fifth category of planning omission confirmed in cycle 15
The planning conversation's failure mode extended to risks never opened as topics, not only questions opened but unresolved

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed elaboration of all eight unknown unknown categories — each could be a full planning conversation. Only the most actionable were developed in depth.
declared_losses.why: practical_constraint — full elaboration of eight risk categories at implementation depth exceeds what is useful at planning closure

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific Pact contract testing implementation guidance — mentioned as the right tool for integration test gap but not elaborated
declared_losses.why: practical_constraint — implementation detail premature until extraction begins

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Shadow mode deployment architecture specifics — traffic duplication patterns, response comparison tooling, divergence alerting
declared_losses.why: practical_constraint — relevant but premature until extraction sequence is confirmed

open_questions: What does the CEO actually mean by 'independent deployment' — per-artifact deployment (strict) or isolated change scope with faster deployment (practical)? Determines destination architecture. Highest priority single question.
Does modular monolith restructuring of auth trigger the 6-week security audit requirement? Compliance officer call — same conversation as session store question.
Does session store migration trigger the audit requirement? Same compliance officer call — overdue since cycle 6.
Can auth security audit and PCI QSA be combined or run concurrently? Same call — three compliance questions, one conversation.

Do payments and inventory currently share a single ACID transaction boundary? New critical output required from seam analysis — determines saga design scope and modular monolith shared-database viability.

Is the payment gateway API call currently inside or outside the database transaction boundary in the monolith? Seam analysis and code inspection — determines whether payments team's consistency concern is based on a real or illusory guarantee.
Is payments coupling to inventory read-only or write/trigger? Seam analysis — unexecuted since cycle 2, now 13 cycles overdue.
Are session tokens co-located or schema-entangled in payments DB? Schema inspection — unexecuted since cycle 3.
What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: shared entities (A), lifecycle hooks (B), or framework usage only (C)? Code inspection with team lead — unexecuted since cycle 5.
Is raw card data stored in monolith or is tokenization provider already in use? Direct codebase and infrastructure audit.
What is the behavioral test coverage of payments, auth, and inventory code? Coverage assessment — newly identified in cycle 15, must be added to archaeology task list.
Who holds critical implicit system knowledge and what happens if they are unavailable? Knowledge concentration audit — newly identified in cycle 15.
instructions_for_next: Cycle 15 closes the primary planning conversation. The unknown unknowns strand adds two new archaeology tasks (test coverage, knowledge concentration) to the existing three (seam analysis, schema inspection, ORM coupling). All five are read-only, all five are executable immediately, all five have been either deferred or never opened. Branch predictions: (1) Conversation concludes — 15 cycles is a complete planning arc from baseline through unknown unknowns. (2) User asks for a consolidated action list — the immediate priorities are the five archaeology tasks plus the three compliance/CEO conversations. (3) User asks about a specific unknown unknown in depth — live data migration mechanics and implicit coupling are the most likely follow-up topics. (4) Implementation conversation begins — first questions will be about which archaeology task to start first or how to structure the test coverage assessment. (5) User asks whether the conversation has been useful given how many things remained unresolved — the honest answer is that the planning work identified the right questions even where it couldn't answer them, and the unresolved questions are a map of what must be resolved before execution, not evidence of planning failure. Key framing for any continuation: the planning phase is complete. The remaining unknowns are execution unknowns resolvable by inspection, not by further planning. The next conversation should be about doing.
feedback_to_harness: <parameter name="process_observations">["Cycle 15 introduced a structurally different type of content — unknown unknowns by definition cannot be derived from prior tensor state, they require generating novel risk categories from outside the conversation's established threads. This felt like a different cognitive operation than integration of new information into existing strands.", "The tensor has now accumulated 15 cycles with strands covering: baseline, sequencing, compliance (PCI + audit), coupling analysis, team dynamics, incident history, greenfield vs extraction, compliance audit constraints, inventory strategy, timeline assessment, MVM definition, success determinant, distributed consistency, architecture pattern validity, epistemic revision, executive communication, and unknown unknowns. This is at genuine saturation — further cycles would require consolidation before new content.", "The harness flag at cycle 14 about tensor size (~5,122 tokens) prompted consolidation of the five strands carried forward. Cycle 15 adds two new strands (Unknown Unknowns, updated Epistemic Revision Log) and updates two existing ones. The tensor is growing again despite consolidation effort — the unknown unknowns content is genuinely new and cannot be compressed into existing strands without loss.", "A pattern across all 15 cycles: the open_questions list has been the most reliable signal of planning health. Its length and staleness (how many cycles each question has been open) is a better indicator of migration risk than any individual strand content."]
